title: Determine Applicable Travel Rule Threshold by Jurisdiction
description: Before processing any crypto-asset transfer, determine which Travel Rule threshold applies based on the jurisdiction of the originating VASP, the jurisdiction of the beneficiary VASP, and the jurisdictions in which the customer is resident. Different jurisdictions apply different monetary thresholds that trigger full Travel Rule data collection and transmission obligations. The United States applies a threshold of USD 3,000 under FinCEN regulations at 31 CFR 1010.410(f). The European Union applies a zero threshold under the Transfer of Funds Regulation recast (Regulation 2023/1113) — all crypto-asset transfers require originator and beneficiary information regardless of amount. The United Kingdom applies a GBP 1,000 threshold under the Money Laundering and Terrorist Financing (Amendment) Regulations 2022. Singapore applies a SGD 1,500 threshold under MAS Notice PSN02. The UAE (VARA) applies a zero threshold — all transfers require Travel Rule compliance. The FATF recommends a USD/EUR 1,000 threshold under Recommendation 16. Where a transfer involves multiple jurisdictions with different thresholds, apply the lower threshold (most restrictive requirement). Maintain a jurisdiction threshold reference table and update it as regulations change. Train operations staff on threshold identification for each supported currency and jurisdiction pair.
threshold_triggers: All outgoing crypto-asset transfers
All incoming crypto-asset transfers
systems: Currency conversion API for threshold comparison
risk_indicators: Transfer structured to fall below applicable threshold
Conflicting jurisdiction determinations for originator and beneficiary
Customer frequently transfers amounts just below the threshold

title: Collect Originator Information — Ordering VASP Obligations
description: As the ordering (originating) VASP, collect the required originator information before initiating a crypto-asset transfer. For transfers at or above the applicable Travel Rule threshold, collect and verify the originator's full name (matching verified KYC records), the originator's account number or unique transaction reference number, and at least one of the following: the originator's physical address, national identity number, customer identification number, or date and place of birth. For transfers below the applicable threshold (where a threshold exists), collect at minimum the originator's name and account number — verification is not required unless suspicious activity is suspected. For EU and UAE transfers (zero threshold), full originator information must be collected for all transfers regardless of amount. Verify the originator information against the KYC records on file — do not rely solely on customer-provided information at the point of transfer. Where the originator is not an existing customer (occasional transaction), conduct CDD appropriate to the transfer amount and risk level before processing. Ensure the originator information is accurate, complete, and recorded in a format that can be transmitted to the beneficiary VASP through the selected Travel Rule protocol.
threshold_triggers: All outgoing transfers at or above applicable threshold
EU/UAE: all outgoing transfers regardless of amount
documents_required: Originator name, account number, address/DOB/ID number
regulatory_basis: US: 31 CFR 1010.410(f)(2); EU: TFR Art. 4; UK: MLR Reg. 64A; SG: PSN02 Para. 13A; AE: VARA AML/CFT Rulebook Ch. 5
risk_indicators: Originator information on file is outdated or incomplete
Originator provides different information at point of transfer vs KYC records
Originator requests transfer to a jurisdiction where they have no known connections

title: Collect Beneficiary Information — Ordering VASP Obligations
description: As the ordering VASP, collect the required beneficiary information before initiating a crypto-asset transfer. For transfers at or above the applicable threshold, collect the beneficiary's full name and the beneficiary's account number at the beneficiary VASP or unique transaction reference. For EU transfers (under the TFR), additional beneficiary information is required for all transfers regardless of amount. The ordering VASP must verify the beneficiary information to the extent possible — while the ordering VASP may not be able to independently verify the beneficiary's identity (as the beneficiary is a customer of the beneficiary VASP), it should check for obvious errors, inconsistencies, or red flags such as the beneficiary name matching a sanctions list entry. Screen the beneficiary name and, where available, wallet address against applicable sanctions lists before processing the transfer. Where the beneficiary is at a VASP in a jurisdiction with known AML/CFT deficiencies, apply enhanced scrutiny. Record all beneficiary information collected in the transaction record. Where the customer is unable or unwilling to provide required beneficiary information, the ordering VASP must not process the transfer and must consider whether the inability to provide information is itself a suspicious activity indicator warranting a SAR filing.
threshold_triggers: All outgoing transfers at or above applicable threshold
EU/UAE: all outgoing transfers regardless of amount
documents_required: Beneficiary account number or transaction reference
regulatory_basis: US: 31 CFR 1010.410(f)(2); EU: TFR Art. 4; UK: MLR Reg. 64A; SG: PSN02 Para. 13A; AE: VARA AML/CFT Rulebook Ch. 5
risk_indicators: Customer cannot provide beneficiary name
Beneficiary name matches sanctions list entry
Beneficiary account is at a VASP in a high-risk jurisdiction
Multiple transfers to the same beneficiary account from different originators
Customer provides clearly fictitious beneficiary information

title: Identify and Verify the Beneficiary VASP
description: Before transmitting Travel Rule data, identify and verify the beneficiary VASP to ensure it is a legitimate, registered or licensed virtual asset service provider. Determine the identity of the beneficiary VASP through the destination wallet address — use blockchain analytics tools and VASP directories to identify which VASP controls the destination address. Verify the beneficiary VASP's regulatory status by checking the VASP against public regulatory registers such as the ESMA register (EU), FCA register (UK), MAS register (Singapore), VARA licensed entities list (UAE), and FinCEN MSB registrations (US). Where possible, verify that the beneficiary VASP has implemented Travel Rule compliance through a compatible protocol. If the beneficiary VASP cannot be identified (the destination is an unhosted wallet), apply the unhosted wallet procedures instead. If the beneficiary VASP is identified but is not registered or licensed in any jurisdiction, apply enhanced risk assessment and consider whether the transfer should proceed. Document the VASP identification and verification process for each transfer. Maintain a database of verified VASP counterparties to streamline future transfers to the same institution.
threshold_triggers: All outgoing transfers to external addresses
First transfer to a new beneficiary VASP
regulatory_basis: EU: TFR Art. 7 (beneficiary VASP obligations); FATF: R.16 INR.16 (counterparty VASP verification)
risk_indicators: Destination address cannot be attributed to any known VASP
Beneficiary VASP is not registered or licensed
Beneficiary VASP is in a jurisdiction with no Travel Rule implementation
Beneficiary VASP has been subject to regulatory enforcement
Beneficiary VASP operates in a sanctioned jurisdiction

title: Select and Configure Travel Rule Protocol
description: Select and implement a Travel Rule protocol for the secure transmission of originator and beneficiary information between VASPs. Multiple Travel Rule protocols have been developed by the industry to facilitate compliance, and VASPs should support one or more of these protocols. TRISA (Travel Rule Information Sharing Architecture) is an open-source protocol developed by the TRISA Working Group that uses mTLS certificates for VASP authentication and encrypted peer-to-peer messaging. OpenVASP is an open-source protocol using decentralised identity and end-to-end encryption. Sygna Bridge (by CoolBitX) is a proprietary solution widely used in Asia-Pacific markets. Notabene is a commercial Travel Rule compliance platform that supports interoperability across multiple protocols. TRP (Travel Rule Protocol by UK-based 21 Analytics) is used primarily in European markets. The Travel Rule Universal Solution Technology (TRUST) was developed by a consortium of US exchanges including Coinbase and Gemini. When configuring the protocol, ensure compliance with data protection requirements (GDPR in EU/UK, PDPA in Singapore) regarding the secure transmission and storage of personal data. Implement encryption at rest and in transit for all Travel Rule data. Test connectivity with counterparty VASPs before processing live transfers. Maintain fallback procedures for cases where the counterparty VASP does not support any compatible protocol.
threshold_triggers: Initial implementation of Travel Rule compliance
Onboarding new counterparty VASPs
Protocol updates or changes
regulatory_basis: FATF: R.16 (Travel Rule); EU: TFR Art. 4-8; US: 31 CFR 1010.410(f); All jurisdictions require secure transmission
risk_indicators: Counterparty VASP does not support any Travel Rule protocol
Data transmitted in unencrypted format
Protocol implementation does not capture all required data fields
No interoperability with major counterparty VASPs
Data protection requirements not addressed in protocol configuration

title: Transmit Travel Rule Data to Beneficiary VASP
description: Transmit the collected originator and beneficiary information to the beneficiary VASP simultaneously with or before the crypto-asset transfer is executed on the blockchain. The data package transmitted must include all required originator information (name, account number, address/DOB/ID), all required beneficiary information (name, account number), the transaction amount and currency/asset type, the date and time of the transaction, and a unique transaction identifier linking the Travel Rule data to the on-chain transaction. The transmission must be executed through the configured Travel Rule protocol with end-to-end encryption. Log the transmission including timestamp, data fields transmitted, the protocol used, and the response received from the beneficiary VASP (acknowledgement, rejection, or no response). Where the beneficiary VASP acknowledges receipt, record the acknowledgement. Where the beneficiary VASP rejects the data (e.g., due to missing or inconsistent information), investigate and resolve the discrepancy before proceeding with the transfer. Where no response is received within a reasonable timeframe, attempt re-transmission and, if unsuccessful, apply the sunrise issue handling procedures. Do not execute the on-chain transfer until Travel Rule data has been successfully transmitted or appropriate risk-based fallback procedures have been applied.
threshold_triggers: All outgoing transfers requiring Travel Rule compliance
documents_required: Transaction reference linking data to on-chain transfer
systems: Selected Travel Rule protocol (TRISA, Notabene, etc.)
regulatory_basis: US: 31 CFR 1010.410(f); EU: TFR Art. 4-5; UK: MLR Reg. 64A; SG: PSN02 Para. 13A; AE: VARA AML/CFT Rulebook
risk_indicators: Beneficiary VASP consistently rejects Travel Rule data
Beneficiary VASP does not acknowledge receipt
Significant delay between data transmission and on-chain transfer execution
Data transmission fails repeatedly for specific counterparty

title: Receive and Verify Travel Rule Data — Beneficiary VASP Obligations
description: As the beneficiary (receiving) VASP, receive and verify Travel Rule data transmitted by the ordering VASP for incoming crypto-asset transfers. Upon receiving Travel Rule data through the configured protocol, verify the completeness of the data package — ensure all required originator and beneficiary fields are populated. Verify that the beneficiary information matches the customer records on file — the beneficiary name and account number in the Travel Rule data must correspond to an active customer account. Screen the originator information against applicable sanctions lists (OFAC SDN, EU Consolidated List, UK Consolidated List, etc.). If the originator name matches a sanctioned entity, do not credit the transfer to the beneficiary's account and escalate to compliance. Assess whether the originator or the transfer itself presents any suspicious activity indicators. For transfers above applicable thresholds, verify the originator's identity information is consistent and plausible. Under EU TFR requirements, the beneficiary VASP must verify the beneficiary's identity before making the crypto-assets available where the transfer exceeds EUR 1,000. If Travel Rule data is missing, incomplete, or inconsistent, do not make the funds available to the beneficiary until the data issue is resolved. Document all received Travel Rule data and verification outcomes.
threshold_triggers: All incoming transfers with Travel Rule data
All incoming transfers above applicable threshold where data is expected
regulatory_basis: US: 31 CFR 1010.410(f)(3); EU: TFR Art. 7-8; UK: MLR Reg. 64B; SG: PSN02 Para. 13B; AE: VARA AML/CFT Rulebook
risk_indicators: Originator name matches sanctions list
Originator information is clearly fictitious or implausible
Travel Rule data does not match the on-chain transfer details
Beneficiary account does not exist
Transfer received from a VASP in a non-compliant jurisdiction

title: Handle Missing Travel Rule Data — Incoming Transfers
description: Implement risk-based procedures for handling incoming crypto-asset transfers where Travel Rule data is missing or incomplete. This situation arises frequently due to the sunrise issue (counterparty VASPs not yet Travel Rule compliant) and when transfers originate from jurisdictions without Travel Rule requirements. When Travel Rule data is missing or incomplete for an incoming transfer above the applicable threshold, do not immediately credit the assets to the beneficiary's account. Attempt to obtain the missing information by contacting the ordering VASP through available channels (Travel Rule protocol, direct communication). If the ordering VASP cannot be identified or does not respond, conduct a risk assessment considering the amount of the transfer, the risk profile of the beneficiary customer, the jurisdiction of origin (based on blockchain analysis), and any other available information about the originator. Based on the risk assessment, determine whether to accept the transfer (for lower-risk situations with documented rationale), hold the transfer pending receipt of information (with time limit), or reject the transfer and return the assets to the originating address. Document the decision, rationale, and any follow-up actions. For EU VASPs under the TFR, there is less flexibility — the beneficiary VASP must not make the crypto-assets available to the beneficiary if originator information is missing for transfers of any amount, until the missing information is obtained.
threshold_triggers: Incoming transfer above threshold with missing or incomplete Travel Rule data
EU: any incoming transfer with missing data
regulatory_basis: EU: TFR Art. 8 (strict — cannot make available without data); FATF: INR.16 (risk-based approach); US: FinCEN guidance; UK: JMLSG guidance
risk_indicators: Repeated transfers from same source without Travel Rule data
Large value transfer with no originator information
Transfer from a jurisdiction with known Travel Rule non-compliance
Beneficiary customer is high-risk
On-chain analysis shows originator address has high-risk exposure

title: Unhosted (Self-Custodial) Wallet Procedures — Outgoing Transfers
description: Implement specific procedures for outgoing crypto-asset transfers to unhosted wallets (also called self-custodial or non-custodial wallets) where there is no beneficiary VASP to receive Travel Rule data. When a customer requests a transfer to an unhosted wallet, the ordering VASP cannot transmit Travel Rule data because there is no institutional counterparty. Different jurisdictions treat this situation differently. Under the EU TFR, for transfers exceeding EUR 1,000 to an unhosted wallet, the VASP must collect originator and beneficiary information as usual and verify that the unhosted wallet is controlled by the customer (i.e., the customer is sending to their own wallet) or identify the person who controls the wallet. Under US FinCEN regulations, the ordering VASP must still collect and retain originator and beneficiary information for transfers of $3,000 or more, even if the beneficiary is an unhosted wallet. Under VARA regulations, all transfers to unhosted wallets require a risk assessment. Best practice across all jurisdictions includes requiring the customer to declare the ownership of the destination wallet, conducting blockchain analysis on the destination address for risk exposure, implementing wallet ownership verification (e.g., Satoshi test or message signing where feasible), and applying enhanced monitoring to customers who frequently withdraw to unhosted wallets. Document all unhosted wallet transfer procedures and risk assessments.
threshold_triggers: Any outgoing transfer to a non-VASP wallet address
EU: enhanced requirements for transfers >EUR 1,000 to unhosted wallets
documents_required: Risk assessment for unhosted wallet transfer
regulatory_basis: EU: TFR Art. 14 (unhosted wallet provisions); US: FinCEN proposed rule on unhosted wallets; UK: MLR regulations; SG: PSN02; AE: VARA AML/CFT Rulebook
risk_indicators: Customer withdraws to unhosted wallet immediately after deposit (pass-through)
Destination wallet has high-risk exposure (mixers, darknet)
Customer refuses to declare wallet ownership
Customer provides false ownership declaration
Large volume of withdrawals to different unhosted wallets in short timeframe
Destination wallet subsequently sends to high-risk services

title: Unhosted Wallet Procedures — Incoming Transfers
description: Implement specific procedures for incoming crypto-asset transfers from unhosted wallets where there is no ordering VASP that has transmitted Travel Rule data. When a customer receives a deposit from an unhosted wallet, the beneficiary VASP does not receive Travel Rule data because the transfer was not initiated by a VASP. Apply the following risk-based procedures: conduct blockchain analysis on the source wallet address to assess risk exposure (sanctions, darknet, mixer, ransomware associations). For deposits above applicable thresholds, require the customer to declare the source of the deposited crypto-assets (their own wallet, payment from a third party, mining proceeds, etc.). Where the customer declares the deposit is from their own self-custodial wallet, consider implementing wallet ownership verification through message signing or a small test transaction. Where the customer declares the deposit is from a third party, obtain the third party's identity information and assess the risk. Under EU TFR provisions, for deposits from unhosted wallets exceeding EUR 1,000, the beneficiary VASP must assess whether the unhosted wallet is owned or controlled by the customer. If risk indicators are identified through blockchain analysis or customer declaration, escalate for enhanced due diligence and consider SAR filing. Monitor for patterns of deposits from multiple unhosted wallets which may indicate layering activity.
threshold_triggers: Any incoming deposit from non-VASP wallet address
EU: enhanced requirements for deposits >EUR 1,000 from unhosted wallets
documents_required: Customer declaration of source of funds
regulatory_basis: EU: TFR Art. 14; US: FinCEN (proposed unhosted wallet rule); UK: JMLSG guidance; SG: PSN02; AE: VARA AML/CFT Rulebook
risk_indicators: Source wallet has direct exposure to sanctioned addresses
Source wallet has significant mixer/tumbler usage
Customer cannot explain source of deposit
Deposits from multiple unhosted wallets in rapid succession
Source wallet associated with darknet marketplace
Customer's declared source of funds contradicts blockchain analysis

title: Sunrise Issue Handling — Non-Compliant Counterparty VASPs
description: Implement procedures for handling the sunrise issue — the period during which some VASPs have implemented Travel Rule compliance while others have not, resulting in an inability to exchange Travel Rule data with non-compliant counterparties. The sunrise issue is a global challenge because Travel Rule implementation timelines vary by jurisdiction, and even within jurisdictions, not all VASPs adopt Travel Rule protocols simultaneously. When a transfer is initiated to a beneficiary VASP that has not implemented Travel Rule compliance, the ordering VASP should first attempt to identify whether the counterparty supports any Travel Rule protocol by checking VASP directories maintained by Travel Rule solution providers (Notabene, TRISA, Sygna). If no protocol is available, attempt manual or out-of-band communication (secure email, API, or direct contact) to transmit the required data. If manual transmission is not feasible, conduct a risk assessment and apply risk-based measures. For lower-risk transfers, proceed with the on-chain transfer while retaining all originator and beneficiary information locally and documenting the inability to transmit. For higher-risk transfers, consider delaying or rejecting the transfer until Travel Rule data can be exchanged. Under EU TFR, which is stricter, the ordering VASP may need to reject the transfer if it cannot transmit Travel Rule data. Document all sunrise issue encounters and the measures applied.
threshold_triggers: Outgoing transfer to a VASP that does not support Travel Rule protocols
Incoming transfer from a VASP that has not transmitted Travel Rule data
documents_required: Counterparty VASP Travel Rule capability assessment
Risk assessment for proceeding without data exchange
regulatory_basis: FATF: INR.16 (risk-based approach during sunrise); EU: TFR (strict approach); Jurisdiction-specific regulatory guidance
risk_indicators: Counterparty VASP consistently unable to exchange Travel Rule data
Counterparty VASP in jurisdiction with no Travel Rule implementation
High-risk transfer that cannot be accompanied by Travel Rule data
Pattern of transfers to non-compliant VASPs suggesting regulatory arbitrage

title: Cross-Chain and Layer-2 Transfer Handling
description: Implement procedures for Travel Rule compliance when processing cross-chain transfers (transfers between different blockchain networks) and Layer-2 transfers (transfers on scaling solutions built on top of base blockchains). Cross-chain transfers present unique Travel Rule challenges because the originating address on one blockchain and the destination address on another blockchain may not be directly linkable through on-chain analysis. When processing cross-chain transfers, ensure that Travel Rule data is associated with the correct transaction identifier for each blockchain involved. Where a cross-chain bridge is used, determine whether the bridge operator is a VASP subject to Travel Rule obligations — if so, Travel Rule data must be exchanged with the bridge operator as an intermediary. For Layer-2 transfers (Lightning Network for Bitcoin, Arbitrum or Optimism for Ethereum, etc.), determine the Travel Rule obligations based on whether the transfer involves a VASP counterparty on the Layer-2 network. Maintain accurate records that link the Travel Rule data to the specific transaction across all involved chains or layers. Blockchain analytics tools are continuously expanding their cross-chain tracing capabilities, and the firm should ensure its monitoring covers all chains and layers on which it operates.
threshold_triggers: Any cross-chain transfer above applicable Travel Rule threshold
Any Layer-2 transfer above applicable threshold
documents_required: Travel Rule data linked to each chain's transaction ID
regulatory_basis: FATF: R.16 applies to all VA transfers regardless of technology; EU: TFR applies to all crypto-asset transfers; Jurisdiction-specific guidance on bridge and L2 treatment
risk_indicators: Cross-chain transfer used to obfuscate fund trail
Transfer routed through multiple bridges without business rationale
Layer-2 transfer to unidentified counterparty
Bridge operator is not a registered/licensed VASP

title: Data Privacy and Protection in Travel Rule Compliance
description: Ensure that all Travel Rule data collection, storage, transmission, and processing complies with applicable data protection legislation including the EU General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR), UK Data Protection Act 2018, Singapore Personal Data Protection Act 2012 (PDPA), and UAE Federal Decree-Law No. 45 of 2021. Travel Rule compliance requires the processing of personal data (names, addresses, dates of birth, identification numbers) which triggers data protection obligations. Identify the lawful basis for processing Travel Rule data — in most jurisdictions, the lawful basis is compliance with a legal obligation (GDPR Art. 6(1)(c)) rather than consent, since Travel Rule compliance is mandatory. Implement data minimisation — collect and transmit only the personal data specifically required by Travel Rule regulations, not additional information. Ensure Travel Rule data is encrypted at rest (AES-256) and in transit (TLS 1.2+). Implement access controls limiting Travel Rule data access to authorised compliance personnel. Conduct a Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA) for the Travel Rule processing activity. Establish data retention policies aligned with both AML record-keeping requirements and data protection principles — retain Travel Rule data for the required AML period (5-8 years) and delete after the period expires. Where Travel Rule data is transmitted to VASPs in jurisdictions without adequate data protection, implement appropriate safeguards such as standard contractual clauses.
threshold_triggers: Implementation of Travel Rule compliance
Processing of any Travel Rule personal data
Cross-border data transfer
documents_required: Privacy notice addressing Travel Rule data
regulatory_basis: EU: GDPR Art. 6(1)(c), Art. 35; UK: DPA 2018; SG: PDPA; AE: Federal Decree-Law No. 45 of 2021; FATF: R.16 guidance on data protection
risk_indicators: Travel Rule data transmitted in unencrypted format
Travel Rule data accessible to unauthorised personnel
No DPIA conducted for Travel Rule processing
Travel Rule data retained beyond required period
Cross-border data transfer without appropriate safeguards

title: Travel Rule Compliance Monitoring and Quality Assurance
description: Implement ongoing monitoring and quality assurance processes to ensure the firm's Travel Rule compliance remains effective and accurate. Conduct regular reviews of Travel Rule data quality by sampling completed transfers and verifying that all required data fields were collected, verified, and transmitted correctly. Monitor the success rate of Travel Rule data transmissions to counterparty VASPs — track acknowledgement rates, rejection rates, and non-response rates by counterparty and protocol. Investigate and resolve patterns of transmission failures. Conduct periodic reconciliation between on-chain transfers and associated Travel Rule data records to ensure every qualifying transfer has corresponding Travel Rule data. Monitor regulatory developments across all applicable jurisdictions for changes to Travel Rule requirements, thresholds, or data fields. Review and update the jurisdiction threshold reference table at least quarterly. Produce regular Travel Rule compliance reports for the MLRO and board including the volume and value of transfers subject to Travel Rule, compliance rates (percentage of qualifying transfers with complete Travel Rule data), top counterparty VASPs by volume and their compliance status, unhosted wallet transfer volumes and risk assessments, and sunrise issue encounters and resolutions. Include Travel Rule compliance in the annual independent AML programme review.
threshold_triggers: Monthly quality assurance sampling
Quarterly compliance reporting
Annual independent review
regulatory_basis: All jurisdictions: ongoing compliance monitoring is implied by the requirement to implement and maintain Travel Rule compliance
risk_indicators: Low Travel Rule compliance rate (<90% of qualifying transfers)
High rejection rate from counterparty VASPs
Significant discrepancies in reconciliation
Outdated threshold table not reflecting recent regulatory changes
Travel Rule not included in annual AML audit scope

title: Batch Transfer and Aggregation Analysis
description: Implement procedures to identify and apply Travel Rule requirements to batch transfers and aggregated transactions. Some customers or institutional counterparties may submit multiple transfers in a single batch or in rapid succession. Where individual transfers fall below the Travel Rule threshold but are conducted in a pattern that suggests they are related or part of a larger transaction, apply aggregation analysis to determine whether the combined value triggers Travel Rule obligations. Under US FinCEN regulations, multiple transmittals of funds by or on behalf of the same person on the same day that aggregate to $3,000 or more trigger Travel Rule requirements. Similar aggregation principles apply in other jurisdictions. Configure transaction monitoring systems to detect potential structuring of transfers to avoid Travel Rule thresholds — multiple transfers just below the threshold to the same or related beneficiary addresses within a short timeframe. Where aggregation triggers are met, collect and transmit full Travel Rule data for the aggregated transaction set. Document the aggregation analysis methodology and ensure it is applied consistently. Report potential structuring patterns to the compliance team for investigation and potential SAR filing.
threshold_triggers: Multiple transfers by same customer in same day
Batch transfer submissions
Transfers just below threshold amounts
documents_required: Travel Rule data for aggregated transfers
regulatory_basis: US: 31 CFR 1010.410(f) (aggregation for same-day transfers); FATF: R.16 guidance on structuring
risk_indicators: Multiple transfers just below threshold in rapid succession
Same originator sending to multiple addresses at same VASP
Transfers split across different crypto-assets to avoid threshold
Customer previously flagged for structuring

title: Intermediary VASP Obligations in Multi-Hop Transfers
description: Implement Travel Rule obligations when the firm acts as an intermediary VASP in a multi-hop transfer chain. An intermediary institution is one that receives and transmits a crypto-asset transfer on behalf of the ordering VASP and the beneficiary VASP without being a party to the underlying transaction. Under FATF Recommendation 16 and implementing regulations, intermediary VASPs must ensure that all Travel Rule information accompanying a transfer is retained with the transfer throughout the payment chain. The intermediary VASP must not strip, alter, or omit originator or beneficiary information from the Travel Rule data package. Where the intermediary receives a transfer without accompanying Travel Rule data, it should apply risk-based procedures — for EU intermediaries, the transfer must not be processed further without the required data. The intermediary should maintain records of all Travel Rule data received and transmitted. Screen originator and beneficiary information against sanctions lists at the intermediary stage. If a sanctions match is identified, halt the transfer and file appropriate reports. Ensure the firm's systems support pass-through of Travel Rule data without data loss or corruption when acting as an intermediary.
threshold_triggers: Firm acts as intermediary in a crypto-asset transfer
Transfer passes through the firm's systems en route to final beneficiary
regulatory_basis: US: 31 CFR 1010.410(f)(4) (intermediary obligations); EU: TFR Art. 10; FATF: R.16
risk_indicators: Travel Rule data stripped or incomplete at intermediary stage
Sanctions match identified at intermediary point
Intermediary processing transfers from non-compliant VASPs
High volume of pass-through transfers suggesting money transmission activity

title: Jurisdiction-Specific Travel Rule Implementation Status Tracking
description: Maintain a comprehensive and up-to-date tracking system for Travel Rule implementation status across all jurisdictions relevant to the firm's operations. The Travel Rule implementation landscape changes frequently as jurisdictions adopt, amend, and enforce their regulations. Track the following for each jurisdiction: whether the jurisdiction has enacted Travel Rule legislation, the effective date of the legislation, the applicable monetary threshold, the specific data fields required (which may exceed FATF minimums), whether the jurisdiction has enforcement mechanisms for Travel Rule non-compliance, the jurisdiction's approach to unhosted wallets, and the jurisdiction's stance on the sunrise issue. Primary sources for tracking include FATF mutual evaluation reports, jurisdiction-specific regulatory publications, industry associations (Global Digital Finance, Chamber of Digital Commerce), and Travel Rule solution provider updates. Update the tracking system at least quarterly and whenever a significant regulatory development occurs. Use the tracking data to inform the firm's risk-based approach to handling transfers involving jurisdictions at different stages of Travel Rule implementation. Share relevant updates with the compliance team and train staff on jurisdiction-specific requirements.
threshold_triggers: Quarterly update cycle
New regulatory announcement in any tracked jurisdiction
FATF plenary or mutual evaluation publication
systems: Regulatory monitoring service (e.g., Thomson Reuters Regulatory Intelligence)
regulatory_basis: FATF: R.16 (global standard); implementation varies by jurisdiction
risk_indicators: Firm's jurisdiction tracker is outdated
Transfers processed to jurisdictions without current implementation assessment
Staff unaware of recent Travel Rule changes in key jurisdictions
Firm applies uniform approach regardless of jurisdiction-specific requirements

title: Travel Rule Data Reconciliation with On-Chain Transactions
description: Implement a systematic reconciliation process to ensure that every crypto-asset transfer subject to Travel Rule requirements has corresponding Travel Rule data, and that the Travel Rule data accurately matches the on-chain transaction. The reconciliation process should run daily (or more frequently for high-volume operations) and should identify any gaps where a qualifying transfer was processed without corresponding Travel Rule data. For outgoing transfers, reconcile the Travel Rule transmission log against the blockchain transaction log to verify that every transfer above the applicable threshold has a Travel Rule data package with a matching transaction hash, amount, and timestamp. For incoming transfers, reconcile received Travel Rule data against deposit records to verify that Travel Rule data has been received for all qualifying incoming transfers and that the data matches the on-chain deposit details. Investigate and resolve any discrepancies identified through reconciliation. Common causes of discrepancies include system errors, timing differences between on-chain confirmation and Travel Rule data transmission, threshold calculation errors due to currency conversion timing, and human error in manual processes. Produce reconciliation reports and escalate unresolved gaps to the compliance team. The reconciliation process should be included in the firm's internal controls framework and subject to periodic testing by internal audit.
threshold_triggers: Daily reconciliation process
End-of-day batch reconciliation
Monthly comprehensive reconciliation review
regulatory_basis: All jurisdictions: implied by requirement to maintain accurate records and ensure Travel Rule compliance
risk_indicators: Reconciliation identifies gaps in Travel Rule coverage
Systematic discrepancies suggesting system misconfiguration
High volume of unresolved gaps
Reconciliation not performed for extended period

title: Travel Rule Incident Response and Breach Handling
description: Implement incident response procedures for Travel Rule compliance failures, data breaches involving Travel Rule data, and regulatory inquiries regarding Travel Rule compliance. Travel Rule incidents include failure to collect required originator or beneficiary information for qualifying transfers, failure to transmit Travel Rule data to the beneficiary VASP, transmission of incorrect or incomplete Travel Rule data, data breach involving Travel Rule personal data, sanctions screening failure at the Travel Rule stage, and regulatory inquiry or examination finding regarding Travel Rule compliance. When a Travel Rule incident is identified, immediately assess the scope and severity of the incident, including the number of affected transfers, the jurisdictions involved, and whether the incident involves sanctions compliance failures. Implement immediate remediation to prevent further non-compliance. For data breaches involving personal data, comply with applicable data breach notification requirements (GDPR requires notification within 72 hours). Conduct a root cause analysis to determine why the incident occurred and implement corrective measures. Document the incident, remediation actions, and root cause analysis. Report material Travel Rule compliance incidents to the MLRO and board. Where the incident constitutes a regulatory breach, consider whether proactive disclosure to the relevant regulator is appropriate — in many jurisdictions, proactive disclosure is viewed favourably and may mitigate penalties.
threshold_triggers: Identification of any Travel Rule compliance failure
Data breach involving Travel Rule data
Regulatory inquiry regarding Travel Rule
Audit finding on Travel Rule deficiency
documents_required: Remediation plan and evidence of implementation
regulatory_basis: EU: GDPR Art. 33-34 (breach notification); All jurisdictions: regulatory expectation of incident management
risk_indicators: Repeated Travel Rule incidents of the same type
Incidents involving sanctioned parties
Delayed incident identification
Inadequate root cause analysis leading to recurrence
Failure to notify regulator of material breach

title: Travel Rule Staff Training and Awareness
description: Develop and deliver targeted training on Travel Rule requirements for all staff involved in processing crypto-asset transfers, compliance, and customer-facing operations. Training content must cover the regulatory basis for the Travel Rule across all applicable jurisdictions, the firm's specific Travel Rule policies and procedures, how to identify transfers subject to Travel Rule requirements (threshold identification), how to collect and verify originator and beneficiary information, how to use the firm's Travel Rule protocol and technology, procedures for handling unhosted wallet transfers, procedures for handling the sunrise issue (non-compliant counterparty VASPs), cross-chain and Layer-2 transfer procedures, escalation procedures for missing or incomplete Travel Rule data, and the consequences of Travel Rule non-compliance (regulatory penalties, licence risk). Deliver initial training to all relevant staff upon onboarding and refresher training at least annually. Provide additional targeted training when regulatory changes occur or when compliance monitoring identifies knowledge gaps. Assess staff comprehension through scenario-based testing that presents realistic Travel Rule situations and requires staff to identify the correct procedure. Maintain records of all training delivered, attendees, and assessment results. Include Travel Rule training completion rates in compliance reporting to the board.
threshold_triggers: New staff onboarding
Annual refresher cycle
Regulatory change
Compliance monitoring identifies training gap
regulatory_basis: All jurisdictions: training is a required element of AML/CFT programmes, which encompass Travel Rule compliance
risk_indicators: Staff unable to identify Travel Rule threshold for their jurisdiction
Staff unaware of unhosted wallet procedures
Low training completion rates
Operational errors traceable to knowledge gaps
Staff using workarounds instead of proper Travel Rule procedures
